title: "Middlemarch by George Eliot"
date: 2024-01-30
tags:

author: George Eliot
genre: Victorian Fiction
excerpt: A novel so vast and so human that it resists any summary — you simply have to live inside it.
---

There are novels you read, and there are novels you inhabit. *Middlemarch* belongs firmly to the second category.

George Eliot published this extraordinary work in installments between 1871 and 1872, subtitling it *A Study of Provincial Life* — a description so modest as to be almost comical, given what she actually delivers. What she delivers is nothing less than a complete world: the fictional English Midlands town of Middlemarch, populated with dozens of characters so fully realized that one finishes the novel half-convinced they were real people one has simply forgotten to stay in touch with.

## The Story

The novel interweaves several plots, chief among them the story of Dorothea Brooke — young, idealistic, painfully intelligent — who makes the catastrophic mistake of marrying the elderly scholar Edward Casaubon, believing she is marrying a great mind. She is not. Casaubon is a dried husk of a man, consumed by a scholarly project (*The Key to All Mythologies*) that he suspects, at some level, is worthless.

Alongside Dorothea's story runs that of Tertius Lydgate, a young doctor who arrives in Middlemarch with similarly grand ideals and similarly disastrous romantic judgment. He falls for Rosamond Vincy, who is beautiful, accomplished, and wholly unable to imagine a world that does not revolve around her.

## Why It Matters

What Eliot understands, with painful precision, is the way ordinary life grinds down extraordinary people. Dorothea could have been remarkable. Lydgate could have made important discoveries. Instead they are caught by the mediocre choices that all of us, in our moments of weakness or loneliness, are capable of making.

The final lines of the novel are among the most quietly devastating in the English language, observing that the good done in the world is accomplished by the many people who lived faithfully hidden lives, and rest in unvisited tombs.

There is no comfort in this exactly. But there is something better than comfort: the feeling of being truly, accurately seen.

**Rating:** ✦ ✦ ✦ ✦ ✦
